worth_it_explanation: Video completion and viewability are the two numbers a buyer reads to decide what a video impression is worth, so they sit directly between a publisher's inventory and the rate it can hold. When the player and Magnite disagree on quartile events, or when viewability is not measured on the inventory at all, the buyer values the impression on the lower of the figures it can see and the publisher loses the ability to defend its price. The check is a review of how completion and viewability are tracked and how they reconcile across the player and Magnite, so it costs reading and testing time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a concrete measurement fix.
intro: Video completion and viewability are the quality signals that tell a buyer how much of a video ad actually played and whether it was on screen long enough to count. This subcategory is about whether the player fires the quartile and completion events the Video Ad Serving Template (VAST) defines, whether those events reconcile with what Magnite reports, and whether viewability is measured on the video inventory so a buyer can value the impression and the publisher can defend its rate. These checks are about the integrity of the measurement rather than the creative or the floor price, so they confirm the numbers a buyer reads are the numbers the publisher's inventory actually produced.

audit_checks.title: Video quartile and completion events do not reconcile between the player and Magnite
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When the player and Magnite report different quartile and completion counts, a buyer reads two versions of how the inventory performed and values it on the lower one. The gap also hides a real tracking fault, so the publisher cannot tell whether the inventory is underperforming or whether the events are simply being lost between the player and the sell-side platform.

educate.base:
Video completion is built from a sequence of tracking events. The Video Ad Serving Template (VAST), the IAB Tech Lab standard that carries a video ad from the ad server to the player, defines tracking URLs the player fires as the ad plays. The player calls 'start' when the ad begins, 'firstQuartile' at 25 percent, 'midpoint' at 50 percent, 'thirdQuartile' at 75 percent, and 'complete' when the ad finishes. The Video Through Rate (VTR), the share of starts that reach completion, is derived from those events. A buyer reads VTR and the quartile curve to judge how much attention the inventory holds, so the events have to fire reliably and the counts have to mean the same thing on both sides of the auction.

Magnite sees these events through the integration the publisher runs, whether that is the Demand Manager header-bidding wrapper, a direct Prebid setup, or a server-side path. The player produces the raw playback events. Magnite reports what it received. When the two are wired correctly, the player's completion count and Magnite's completion count track each other closely. When they are not, the numbers drift apart, and the drift is the symptom of a fault that is costing the publisher either accuracy or revenue.

The break usually sits in how the events are captured and passed rather than in the playback itself. A player can fire every quartile correctly while the wrapper drops the events before they reach Magnite, or a server-side path can lose the 'complete' event on the way through. The events can fire on a different timing basis than Magnite expects, so a midpoint on the player is counted somewhere else by the time it reaches the sell-side report. Prebid's own video documentation, which applies to Magnite's Prebid-based Demand Manager, describes how VAST tracking and instream tracking have to be configured for impression and event data to reach the analytics and bidding layers, and a gap in that configuration is a common cause of the mismatch.

A few signs point to quartile and completion events that do not reconcile:
  • The player's completion count and Magnite's completion count for the same inventory and period differ by more than a small margin that timing alone would explain
  • The quartile curve looks intact in the player's own analytics but flattens or breaks in the Magnite report, which suggests events are lost in transit
  • VTR reported to buyers through Magnite is lower than the player measures, so the inventory looks weaker than it performed
You may want to confirm that the quartile and completion events the player fires arrive at Magnite and reconcile within a small margin. One approach is to read completion and quartile counts from the player's analytics and from Magnite reporting for the same inventory and period, then trace any gap back to the integration that carries the events.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, instream video in article and section pages often runs through a single player configuration, so a reconciliation gap on one template repeats across the site. You may want to compare the player's completion count against Magnite for a representative template, then confirm the same wiring is in place on the others rather than checking each page in isolation.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, video that sits alongside product media can compete with heavier page assets for load order, which can delay the player and cost early quartile events. You may want to confirm the player initializes and fires its tracking events on these pages before judging whether a low VTR reflects the inventory or a load-order fault.

learn_more.source_title: Prebid.js for Video Overview for Header Bidding

educate.learn_more_note: Magnite's detailed Demand Manager and player-integration documentation for video event tracking is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Compare the quartile and completion counts the player records against what Magnite reports for the same inventory and period, and trace any gap back to the integration that carries the events.

magnite_console.label: Magnite console and reporting
steps: Open Magnite reporting and build a report for the video inventory in question, broken out with starts, the four quartile events, completions, and Video Through Rate (VTR).
Record the completion count and the quartile counts Magnite reports for a defined inventory slice and date range.

Note whether Magnite is receiving the integration through the Demand Manager wrapper, a direct Prebid setup, or a server-side path since that determines where the events travel before they reach this report.

player_analytics.label: Video player analytics
steps: Open the video player's own analytics or its event log for the same inventory slice and date range used in the Magnite report.
Record the start, quartile, and completion counts the player measured, and derive the player-side VTR.
Compare the player's completion count against the Magnite completion count and note the size and direction of any gap.
Where a gap exists, inspect a live auction in the browser network log to confirm whether the quartile and completion tracking calls fire and reach Magnite.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the start, quartile, and completion counts from the player and from Magnite for the same inventory and period, alongside the integration path that carries the events. Counts that reconcile within a small margin that timing alone explains pass. A material gap, especially one where Magnite reports fewer completions than the player or where the quartile curve breaks in the Magnite report, is a fail recorded with the inventory, period, and the integration point where the events appear to be lost.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the player and Magnite quartile and completion counts for the same inventory and period above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the video quartile and completion events for {{site_name}} do not reconcile between the player and Magnite, so the Video Through Rate (VTR) and quartile curve a buyer reads through Magnite differ from what the player actually measured. The Video Ad Serving Template (VAST) defines the quartile and completion events the player fires, and when those events are lost between the player and the sell-side platform the inventory looks weaker than it performed, which lowers what a buyer will pay. {{voice_recommend}} reading the player and Magnite completion counts for the same inventory and period, tracing any gap back to the integration that carries the events, and correcting the tracking so the counts reconcile within a small margin. After the fix, recheck VTR on both sides to confirm the numbers a buyer reads match the inventory's real performance.

audit_checks.title: Viewability is not measured on the video inventory sold through Magnite
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When viewability is not measured on the video inventory, a buyer cannot tell whether the impression was on screen long enough to count, so it values the inventory cautiously or discounts it against inventory that does report viewability. The publisher also loses the evidence it needs to defend its rate and to separate inventory that genuinely performs from inventory that does not.

educate.base:
Viewability answers a question completion alone cannot. A video ad can play to completion in a tab the user never looks at, so completion tells a buyer the ad ran while viewability tells the buyer the ad had a chance to be seen. The Media Rating Council (MRC) defines a video impression as viewable when at least 50 percent of the ad's pixels are on screen for at least two continuous seconds. A buyer reads viewability alongside Video Through Rate (VTR) to decide what the impression is worth, and inventory that reports viewability competes for higher bids than inventory that reports none.

Measuring viewability on video requires a measurement signal the buyer trusts. That signal comes either from the player reporting MRC-aligned viewability through the integration, or from an accredited third-party verification vendor whose tag runs on the inventory and reports independently. For a publisher monetizing through Magnite, the audit question is whether either signal is present on the video inventory, so a buyer bidding through Magnite can see a viewability figure rather than bidding into inventory that reports nothing on this dimension.

When viewability is not measured, the cost is indirect but real. The inventory is not blocked, but it is valued cautiously. A buyer with a viewability target either avoids the inventory it cannot measure or bids it down to absorb the uncertainty, and the publisher has no number to point to when it argues the inventory performs. Measuring viewability turns a defensive position into an evidence-based one, and it lets the publisher separate the inventory that genuinely sits in view from the inventory that does not.

A few signs point to viewability that is not measured on the video inventory:
  • Magnite reporting for the video inventory shows no viewability metric, or shows it as unmeasured for a large share of impressions
  • No accredited third-party verification tag is present on the player, and the player itself reports no MRC-aligned viewability
  • Buyers raise viewability as a reason for low bids or for avoiding the inventory, and the publisher has no measurement to answer the concern
You may want to confirm that the video inventory carries a viewability signal a buyer can read, whether from the player or from an accredited verification vendor. One approach is to check Magnite reporting for a video viewability metric, then confirm the player or a third-party tag is producing the MRC-aligned measurement that feeds it.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, sticky or floating video players are designed to stay on screen as the reader scrolls, which can produce strong viewability that the publisher cannot claim if nothing measures it. You may want to confirm a viewability signal is present on those placements so the inventory's natural advantage shows up in the numbers a buyer reads.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, video on product and category pages reaches a higher-intent audience, and a viewability figure on that inventory helps justify a higher rate. You may want to confirm viewability is measured on the commercial placements specifically rather than only on the editorial video, so the higher-value inventory carries the evidence to support its price.

educate.learn_more_note: Magnite's detailed documentation for viewability reporting and supported verification vendors is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Check whether the video inventory sold through Magnite reports a viewability metric, and confirm the player or an accredited third-party tag is producing the measurement that feeds it.

magnite_console.label: Magnite console and reporting
steps: Open Magnite reporting for the video inventory and look for a viewability metric, such as measured rate and viewable rate, alongside impressions.
Record what share of video impressions report as measured and what share report as viewable, or note that no viewability metric is available for the inventory.
Note whether viewability is reported by the player through the integration or by an accredited third-party verification vendor since that determines where the signal originates.

player_and_tag_check.label: Player and verification tag check
steps: Inspect a live video placement in the browser network log and check whether the player fires Media Rating Council (MRC) aligned viewability events or an accredited verification vendor's measurement tag.
Confirm the measurement covers the video inventory that runs through Magnite rather than only a subset of placements.
Where no signal is present, record which placements lack viewability measurement so the gap can be addressed at the player or vendor level.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether the video inventory sold through Magnite reports a viewability metric, the source of the measurement, and the share of impressions measured. Video inventory that carries an MRC-aligned viewability signal a buyer can read, from the player or an accredited vendor, across most impressions passes. Video inventory with no viewability measurement, or with a large share reported as unmeasured, is a fail recorded with the placements that lack the signal.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether the video inventory reports a viewability metric and the source of the measurement above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the video inventory {{site_name}} sells through Magnite carries no viewability measurement, so a buyer cannot tell whether the impression was on screen long enough to count. The Media Rating Council (MRC) defines a viewable video impression as at least 50 percent of the ad's pixels on screen for at least two continuous seconds, and a buyer reads that figure alongside completion to decide what the impression is worth, so unmeasured inventory is valued cautiously or discounted. {{voice_recommend}} adding an MRC-aligned viewability signal to the video inventory, whether from the player or an accredited third-party verification vendor, and confirming it reports through Magnite for most impressions. After the change, recheck the viewable rate so {{site_name}} can defend its rate with measurement rather than argue it without.
